meta.purpose: Demonstrate management cognition patterns through concrete cases of upstream diagnosis.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks for examples or case studies.

cases.title: The case of the engineer who was 'not performing' after three performance reviews
cases.context: A manager spent six months documenting an engineer's missed deadlines and quality issues. After three formal performance reviews and a PIP, the engineer was let go. The replacement, hired at higher salary, showed the same pattern within two months.
cases.what_happened: A skip-level investigation revealed that the engineering team's task assignment process had no priority system. Engineers were receiving tasks from three different stakeholders with no mechanism for resolving conflicts. Every engineer on the team was regularly missing deadlines — the fired engineer was just the most visible because they had the most stakeholders.
cases.what_was_learned: The problem was never the person. The system had no priority resolution mechanism. The fired engineer had been saying 'I have too many conflicting requests' in every one-on-one, and it was recorded as 'makes excuses.' The replacement failed the same way because the system was unchanged.
cases.structural_pattern: execution_failure → manager_blames_person → PIP → termination → replacement_hired → same_failure → skip_level_diagnosis → system_fault_found → system_fixed → performance_normalizes

cases.title: The CRM that the team refused to use (and the manager who listened)
cases.context: A sales director rolled out a new CRM system. The team was trained, the system was configured, and a deadline was set. After two months, adoption was under 30%. Team members were maintaining shadow spreadsheets and entering data only when forced.
cases.what_happened: Instead of escalating enforcement, the director asked: 'What is this system asking you to do that you believe makes your work harder?' The team explained: entering data into the CRM added 45 minutes to each sales call. The CRM was designed for pipeline reporting, not for sales workflow. The director worked with IT to redesign the entry flow, reducing data entry from 45 minutes to 8 minutes per call. Adoption went to 95% without any enforcement.
cases.what_was_learned: The team wasn't resisting change. They were resisting pointless work. The CRM was designed for management visibility, not for team utility. When the tool started helping the team instead of just monitoring them, adoption was organic.
cases.structural_pattern: tool_rollout → low_adoption → enforcement_threatened → manager_asks_why_instead_of_demanding → discovers_system_makes_work_harder → redesign_for_utility → organic_adoption_follows

cases.title: Three dead projects that everyone knew were dead but nobody said
cases.context: A product team had three projects in 'green' status on the weekly report. At the quarterly review, all three were irrecoverably behind. The engineering lead admitted: 'We've known for two months these weren't going to make it.'
cases.what_happened: Investigation revealed that the weekly status meeting had become a ritual of defending performance rather than surfacing reality. When one engineer had flagged a risk honestly the previous quarter, they were asked 'whose fault is this' and made to present a 'recovery plan' in front of leadership. The lesson the team learned: green on the report keeps you safe. Red gets you a public interrogation.
cases.what_was_learned: The team wasn't dishonest. They were rational actors in a system that punished honesty. The fix was not 'better communication training.' The fix was a structural change: weekly risk flags were anonymous, no recovery plan required for flagging, and managers were evaluated on how early they surfaced problems, not on how green their reports were.
cases.structural_pattern: problems_known → past_punishment_for_surfacing → greenwashing → quarterly_disaster → honest_diagnosis → anonymous_flagging_system → early_surfacing_rewarded → problems_caught_before_crisis
